content: I figured this was the better place to discuss it as it's only relevant to the English translation? idk
Exactly, which is why I need 2 verbs that sound good together. One Subjective (gain) and one Objective (grant)
I agree for having the two terms to be given for specific context
Using Horla as an example, when her Elegy Psalm is played, she *gains* Aliemus, but when her Carol Psalm is played, she *grants* another Awakener Aliemus.

but, with this context, the most lore-accurate verb to describe an Awakener "gaining" Aliemus would be **Accumulate**, but that's just too long for use on basically half of the cards in the game, so I like **Accrue**.
yes. similar meaning but different outcome.. I did that too on ID
but still, "Another target Awakener accrues (X) Aliemus" sounds way more awkward than "Another target Awakener gains (X) Aliemus", and the gains one is already bad
"Grant another Awakener (X) Ailemus" ?

correct, this is what I'm going with, but I was pairing the objective **Grant** with the subjective **Gain**, and have been getting pushback as it feels kinda weird to have "Gain Shield" and "Gain Aliemus" in the same sentence.

my hope was to find a verb pair that worked as well as grant & gain, but that's a dead end, so I'm lowering my standards lol. I think Grant & Accrue is fine.

and honestly this is kinda giving me a weird, like, pseudo- existential crisis? for real why do we even have an English channel if (a) the entire server is expected to communicate in English outside of their language's channels and (b) we talk about the english translation in General anyway...?

I'm gonna migrate this over there and just stop using this channel until someone tells me otherwise lmao
idk, i much prefer gain shield and gain aliemus to using other words
i kinda don't see why we need different terms for each
going through the texts and I keep laughing at how fed up the keeper is at the awakener antics

**LOL** okay so I'm flipping through the Archive out of curiosity cus I love this game and came across two back-to-back uses of this kinda awkward sounding term
I didn't actually read Miryam's story when I did it, I just wanted the rewards during the event, and wanted to revisit after proofreading went online, so I don't actually know who Deborah even is... But I loved Horla's story and OHHHH MAN
I don't know what the original CN is here so I don't know if this is just a hilariously awkward MTL or if this is legitimate confirmation of the *extreme* "Sappho and her friend" situation between those two 
I've never heard of the term "bosom friend" but I *HAVE* heard "bosom *buddy*" and where I'm from that's a very obvious euphemism with the exact same connotation as "friends with benefits"
you've never heard the term bosom friends?
huh
I wonder if it's a regional thing or something
like I said, I've only heard "bosom buddy"
but for real though like was I hallucinating or were Horla and Colette *extremely homosexual*
it's a fairly standard phrase as far as I'm aware
it just means close friends
makes sense, that's pretty much what I read it as, except with heavy airquotes on """""close"""""
Bosom.....

lots of discussion has been had over verbs, and we haven't come to any consensus yet.
This worldbuilding tidbit makes me feel like the verb we should use for Aliemus is **Accrue**, which we'd had some agreement on using for Keyflare.

I like having Keyflare and Aliemus share a verb as they're very similar mechanics, but my initial gripe with it was that there was no convenient objective verb to use alongside it, like **Gain** has with **Grant**.

Honestly I'm fine with having **Accrue** and **Grant** work together. What do we think folks?

I believe this is better brought in General? for multiple perspectives?

But, here's a perspective from ID language logic..
Grant gives off better contextual translation compared to Gain.
and for specific terms, although unusual in ID, we kept it as it is.
because, in ID logic.. Gain is something that obtained through the subject's effort, Grant is something obtained from external factor
